rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And Or Compliance With Statement Of Compliance (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 4 of 4 RFPs] Petitioners are ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 4 of 4 RFPs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Petitioners Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admissions And Form Interrogatories (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4 RFAs] The pleading underlying this discovery is Petition to Invalidate Trust; for Constructive Trust; for Breach of Trust; to Compel Information, etc., naming Respondent in both her personal capacity and in her capacity as Trustee of the Leon Gomez Chang and Maria C. Gomez Chang Revocable Trust. The Petition seeks, in part, to invalidate the Trust on the basis of Respondent's alleged undue influence over the elderly settlors, Leon and Maria, and alleges Respondent also financially abused Leon and Maria. Respondent has denied the Petition's allegations in the Preliminary Response and Objection. Whether Respondent did or did not have a "confidential relationship" and/or a fiduciary relationship with one or both of the elders during the relevant time periods will be legally significant to the determination and outcome of this conflict. Even if Respondent individually does not have sufficient legal knowledge to admit or deny these requests, her attorneys or expert witnesses reasonably would. The court believes that the failure to at least partially respond to the requests indicates a lack of good faith inquiry, and a failure to provide as complete and straightforward a response as possible. The responses offered are not Code compliant. The parties are also reminded, when responding to RFAs, the failure to admit an issue of "substantial importance" in response to an RFA can result in an award of expenses incurred in proving the issue at trial, "if it has at least some direct relationship to one of the central issues in the case - i.e. an issue which, if not proven, would have altered the results in the case." (Bloxham v. Saldinger (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 729; Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.420(a). Petitioners'' Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admissions and Form Interrogatories is GRANTED. Respondent requests that Petitioners be sanctioned in an amount of no less than $5,500, for failure to meet and confer in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.290(b)(1), arguing that this failure is a sanctionable under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.020. Respondent's request for sanctions is DENIED. The record before the court on this motion demonstrates counsel for both Petitioners and Respondent engaged in good faith meet and confer communication and supplemental production, specifically addressing the four RFAs at issue in this motion. Petitioners' request for sanctions is GRANTED pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 2033.290(d) and 2030.300(d). The court does not find that Respondent acted with substantial justification in opposing this motion. The court does not find that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Respondent Li Yi Lau is ORDERED to pay Petitioners sanctions, in an amount of no more than $7,500, to be determined by the court after hearing on this motion and the concurrently-set motion to compel further responses to Requests for Production of Documents, within 10 days of service of notice of entry of this order. [Part 3 of 4 RFAs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And Or Compliance With Statement Of Compliance (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4 RFPs] Respondent's' request that Petitioners be sanctioned in an amount of no less than $5,500, under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.020, for failure to meet and confer on this issue in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(b)(2), arguing that this failure is a sanctionable "misuse of the discovery process" under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.010(i). Respondent's' request for sanctions under this statutory theory is DENIED, for the following reasons. A propounding party who determines a response to an RFP is incomplete waives any right to compel a further response, if they do not file and serve notice of the motion to compel "within 45 days of the service of the verified response, or any supplemental verified response," unless the parties agree to a specific later date in writing. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(c).) Petitioners' statutory deadline to file a motion to compel further responses to Request For Production of Documents Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77, based on the date of the supplemental verified response, was October 26, 2022. Respondent is incorrect that further meet and confer communication between counsel extends the statutory 45-day period, which is calculated from the date of service of the verified or supplemental verified response. Under the statute, the only exception to the 45 day time limit is "on or before any specific later date to which the demanding party and the responding party have agreed in writing." (CCP section 2031.310(c).) The record shows that Respondent's counsel declined to grant Petitioners' counsel's request to extend the October 26 filing deadline in an October 21, 2022 email. (Zabronsky Decl. Exh. O.) Respondent subsequently served a supplemental privilege log on October 25, 2022 via email, and the transmittal email from Respondent's counsel discussed Petitioners' identification log. (Zabronsky Decl. Exh. Q.) Attorney Zabrosky's responding email the same day indicated he would not have time to discuss further production because the motion filing deadline was the next day. Respondent's counsel's responding email the same day indicated Respondent would not be producing further supplemental written responses to the discovery, but offered to extend the meet and confer period until 15 days after Respondent served her identification log (Zabrinsky Decl. Exh. R.) This offer was apparently accepted. On November 14, 2022, Respondent produced further responsive documents that had been erroneously left out of earlier production. On November 29, 2022, Respondent served the identification log. This motion to compel was filed December 14, 2022, 15 days after Respondent served the identification log. [Part 2 of 4 RFPs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And Or Compliance With Statement Of Compliance (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4 RFPs] There is no showing on this record that the parties engaged in meet and confer communications regarding Petitioners' Request For Production of Documents Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77, or Respondent's responses or corresponding statements of compliance, after October 25, 2022. As Respondent states in the Opposition to the Motion, in the last October 25, 2022 email between Petitioners and Respondent's counsel on this record, Respondent's counsel "expressed that Li Yi would not be further supplementing her written responses to the Discovery Requests" (Opp. at 6:13-19). While Respondent's counsel revised the offer on extending the motion filing deadline in that same email, there is no showing in the record that Respondent's counsel withdrew or revised the statement about no further written responses. The court finds it was not "misuse of the discovery process" under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.010(i) for Petitioners to take statements made by Respondent's counsel, within the meet and confer process and not later refuted, at face value. Petitioners' request for sanctions is GRANTED pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 2031.310 and 2031.320. Under Civ. Proc. Code section 2031.310(h), Except as provided in subdivision (j) [production of electronically stored information], the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel further response to a demand, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Under Civ. Proc. Code section 2031.320(b), Except as provided in subdivision (d) [production of electronically stored information], the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel compliance with a demand, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. The court does not find that Respondent acted with substantial justification in opposing this motion. The Opposition acknowledges Respondent's responses to Petitioners' Request For Production of Documents ("RPD") Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77, and written statements of compliance are defective. The court does not find that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. Respondent Li Yi Lau is ORDERED to pay Petitioners sanctions in the amount of $7,500, within 10 days of service of notice of entry of this order. [Part 3 of 4 RFPs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And Or Compliance With Statement Of Compliance
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4 RFPs] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Petitioners' and Beneficiaries' ("Petitioners") Jeanette Chu, John M. Gomez, Richard Chan, William Gomez, and Ray Gomez's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production of Documents and/or Compliance with Statement of Compliance. Per the Notice of Motion, this motion to compel is brought pursuant to "Code of Civ. Proc. [sections] 2031.310(a) and 2031.320(a), on the grounds that the Responding Party has failed, without justification, to comply with her written representation that she would produce for inspection certain documents and things and continues to fail and refuse to produce said documents and things in violation of Code of Civ. Proc. [section] 2031.010, et. seq." The Notice of Motion then states the motion seeks relief in the alternative, in the form of an order directing Respondent to serve amended verified responses with a complete and accurate statement of compliance and without objections, if Respondent does not actually have responsive documents to produce. Under Civ. Proc. Code section 2031.310(a), On receipt of a response to [a Request for Production], the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. Per the Notice of Motion, Petitioners seek "an order compelling Respondent Li Yi Lau [ ] to provide further responses to Petitioners' Request For Production of Documents [ ] Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77, and/or to comply with her written statement of compliance and permit the inspection and production of documents as to such RPDs." No documents were listed on the document identification log for these Requests for Production ("RFPs"). The privilege log did not indicate that the withheld documents would have corresponded to these RFPs. It was unclear why there was no response to these RPFs: whether Respondent's statement of compliance was incomplete, or the privilege log was incomplete, or the production log was incomplete, or whether more than one of the logs was incomplete. In the Opposition, Respondent states, "she made statements of compliance with respect to the 23 Requests for Production of Documents that are the subjects of the present motion and [ ] her identification log does not identify which of the documents she produced corresponded to those requests," and states her willingness to cure the deficiencies in the identification log. (Opp. at 1:2-7, 9:21-24.) As of the issuance of this tentative ruling, there is no showing on the record that Respondent has served further production or a corrected identification log. Petitioners' Motion Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production of Documents and/or Compliance with Statement of Compliance is GRANTED. Respondent is ORDERED to produce (1) an amended corrected complete identification log to provide further responses to Petitioners' Request For Production of Documents Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77, and (2) amended corrected complete statements of compliance regarding Request For Production of Documents Nos. 8, 9, 10-13, 15, 21-25, 28, 29, 38, 42, 45, 61-63, 69, 71, and 77. Respondent is FURTHER ORDERED to permit the inspection, copying, testing, or sampling in accordance with those statements statement of compliance, per Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.320. [Part 1 of 4 RFPs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioners Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admissions And Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4 RFAs] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court is Petitioners' and Beneficiaries' ("Petitioners") Jeanette Chu, John M. Gomez, Richard Chan, William Gomez, and Ray Gomez's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admissions and Form Interrogatories. Per the Notice of Motion, this motion is brought "pursuant to Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.290(a)(1)-(2), for an order compelling Respondent Li Yi Lau [ ] to provide further responses to Petitioners' Request Admissions, Set One ("RFA") Nos. 110-113. This motion is made on the grounds that the Responding Party's responses to RFA Nos. 110-113 are evasive and/or incomplete and include objections that are without merit or too general." Per the Notice of Motion, this motion is also brought, "pursuant to Code Civ. Proc. sections 2030.300(a)(1) and (3), for an order compelling Li Yi to provide further responses to Petitioners' Form Interrogatories, Set One ("FI") No. 17.1 with respect to RFA Nos. 110-113 on the grounds that Li Yi's responses to RFA Nos. 110-113 are evasive and/or incomplete and include objections that are without merit or too general." RFAs No. 110 and 111 request Respondent admit she had a "confidential relationship," as defined, with her former father in law and mother in law, during a specified period of time. RFAs No. 112 and 113 request Respondent admit she had a "fiduciary relationship" with her former father in law and mother in law, during a specified period of time. Respondent argues in the Opposition to the Motion that her responses to RFA Nos. 110-113 comply with section 2033.2220, in that they are "a statement claiming inability to admit or deny" the RFA, correctly stating that "a reasonable inquiry concerning the matter in the particular request has been made, and that the information known or readily obtainable is insufficient to enable [Respondent] to admit the matter." Correspondingly, Respondent argues her responses to Form Interrogatory 17.1 regarding RFAs 110 through 113 are Code compliant and consistent with her RFA responses, as they also state she lacks sufficient knowledge to admit the request after making a reasonable investigation of the matter. Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.290 authorizes a party propounding RFAs to seek further responses in the event they conclude that the individual responses received are legally insufficient. The propounding party "may move for an order compelling a further response if that party deems that either or both of the following apply: (1) An answer to a particular request is evasive or incomplete. (2) An objection to a particular request is without merit or too general." Petitioners are within their statutory right to seek further responses to these RFAs, which neither admit nor deny the requests. (See also, Code Civ. Proc. section 2017.010.) Absent an objection, the response to an RFA must contain one of the following: An admission; A denial; A statement claiming inability to admit or deny. (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.220(b).) Facially, yes, the disputed responses match one of the response options offered by the Code. However, Respondent's argument that a copy and paste response entirely satisfies her obligations under the Discovery Act, ignores the responding party's other statutory duties when responding to a Request for Admission. [Part 1 of 4 RFAs]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE LEON GOMEZ CHANG AND MARIA C. GOMEZ CHANG REVOCABLE TRUST DATED JUNE 2, 2002
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Petitioners Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admissions And Form Interrogatories (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4 RFAs] The responding party must respond separately to each request for admission, in writing, and under oath (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.210(a)). Each response must either (1) "answer the substance of the requested admission" or (2) "set forth an objection" (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.210(b)). Each answer "shall be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits" and must "[a]dmit so much of the matter involved in the request as is true  or as reasonably and clearly qualified by the responding party." (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.220(a), (b)(1).) If any portion of the RFA is true, the responding party must admit that portion (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.220(b)(1).) If any portion of the RFA is untrue, the responding party may "deny so much of the matter involved in the request as is untrue." (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.220(b)(2).) Similarly, if only part of an RFA is objectionable, the objection should be to that part; the remainder of the request for admission must be answered. (Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.230(a)). It is not proper ground for an objection that the RFA is "ambiguous," unless it is so ambiguous that the responding party cannot in good faith frame an intelligent reply. (See, Cembrook v. Sup.Ct. (Sterling Drug, Inc.) (1961) 56 Cal. 2d 423, 428-431.) Respondents objections on the basis of the request being speculative or vague are belied by her ability to understand them sufficiently to conduct a "reasonable inquiry" and conclude she does not have enough information to either admit or deny the requests. The response to an RFA is not meant to be mechanically limited to only one of the three options listed in Code Civ. Proc. section 2033.220(b). Although a denial of any part or all of an RFA must be "unequivocal," a response may include reasonable qualifications or offer additional relevant information within the substance of the request, so as to be as complete and straightforward as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits. (See, St. Mary v. Sup. Ct. (2014) 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, 780; Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 Cal.App.3d 771, 783.) As explained in case law, "Requests for admissions ... are primarily aimed at setting at rest a triable issue so that it will not have to be tried. Thus, such requests, in a most definite manner, are aimed at expediting the trial. For this reason, the fact that the request is for the admission of a controversial matter, or one involving complex facts, or calls for an opinion, is of no moment. If the litigant is able to make the admission, the time for making it is during discovery procedures, and not at the trial." (Bloxham v. Saldinger (2014) 228 Cal. App. 4th 729, 752.) The Discovery Act requires the responding party to undertake a "good faith" obligation to investigate sources of information reasonably available to them, in formulating answers to RFAs (Civ. Proc. C. section 2033.220(c); see Chodos v. Sup.Ct. (Lowe) (1963) 215 CA2d 318, 322.) Information known to a party's attorney or expert witnesses is "obtainable" by the party, and responses to RFAs must be made in light of that reasonably available source of information. [Part 2 of 4 RFAs]
